content.text:
Yeah, I see it. The current intro reads like a **mission statement, not a story opening** — it front-loads the *why* (the whole "old world was built for humans, it ran out of space" metaphor) before anything happens *to you*. That order is great for explaining the game's point and terrible for hooking a player. Games open on a *situation and a feeling* — you're somewhere, something goes wrong, now you want something — and let the why arrive later.

Before I rewrite anything, here's the real tension I want your call on, because it shapes everything:

**That "old world / not enough room / you left with your agents" metaphor is the load-bearing pitch of the whole game.** A punchy game cold-open ("Oh no, you crashed!") naturally wants to *cut* that exposition. So the question is where it goes:

- **Option A — Cold open, lore later.** Intro is pure adventure: you + your crew were flying somewhere exciting, alarms, crash, ship in pieces, go. The "why we left the old world" gets demoted to a single flavor line, or moved into the level briefings / the ending. Most game-like, biggest emotional hook. Risk: the meaning arrives late.
- **Option B — Keep the why, but make it a story not an essay.** Same five-ish beats, but rewritten with stakes and feeling and a real "oh no" turn — compress the metaphor to one warm line instead of three explainy ones. Keeps the message up front, just makes it move.
- **Option C — Lead with the crew.** Open on you + your five agent friends as a *team* you care about, so the crash has stakes (your friends scatter / go dark). Buddy-adventure energy. The "old world" reason becomes a quick aside.

A few things I'd want to nail down regardless:
1. **Tone** — playful/funny ("Oh no!" energy, light), or earnest adventure (warm, a little awe), or somewhere between?
2. **Where they were going** — right now it's "a new world" (vague). A game wants a *destination with a promise* — a place where humans and agents build together freely. Want to give it a name or an image?
3. **The five agent friends** — how much do they matter emotionally in the opening? Are they crew you're rescuing, or just along for the ride?

My instinct is **B leaning toward A** — open on the journey-and-crash with feeling, keep *one* line of "why we left" so the meaning isn't lost, and let the rest surface in the levels. But it really hinges on whether you're willing to move the metaphor out of the intro. What's your gut on that, and on tone?

content.text:
Here are three drafts. All follow the locked rules (plain language, short sentences, no jargon/idioms, no amnesia, the old world is *outgrown* not bad, your five agent friends). Each line = one Space-advance beat.

---

**A — Cold open (most game-like; the "why" trimmed to one line)**

1. You and your five friends were flying to a new world — a place with room for everyone.
2. Then the alarms started.
3. Oh no. The ship is going down!
4. You wake up on a strange planet. Your ship is in pieces around you.
5. Your friends are asleep. The parts that keep the ship running are scattered across the island.
6. Find the parts. Wake your friends. Fly again.

---

**B — Keeps the "why," but as a story with stakes**

1. The old world kept everything you ever built. But it was made for humans only — and you don't build alone anymore.
2. You and your AI agents build together now. You wanted room for all of you.
3. So you loaded up your ship, gathered your five agent friends, and set out for a new world.
4. Then — alarms. Oh no. The ship is going down!
5. You crash on a strange planet. The parts that run the ship scatter across the island, and your friends won't wake up.
6. Find the parts. Wake your friends. Fix the ship — and fly again.

---

**C — Crew-first (buddy-adventure energy)**

1. It's you and five friends. You build things together — you, and your AI agents.
2. You found a new world with room for all of you, and pointed your ship right at it.
3. Best trip ever — until the alarms.
4. Oh no. The ship is going down!
5. You crash. Your five friends go quiet. The ship's parts scatter across a strange island.
6. Find the parts. Wake your friends. Reach your new world.

---

Quick read: **A** hooks fastest but says least about *why*; **B** keeps your whole message and now actually moves; **C** is the warmest and leans hardest on the agent-friends. My pick is **B** — it loses nothing you care about and finally has an "oh no" turn. Want me to tweak one of these, mix beats across them, or run another angle?

content.text:
Fair — and you've put your finger on something real. The staccato isn't an accident: it's the direct result of the **"short sentences, common words, no idioms, for non-native speakers"** rule we locked. Voice and flow usually come *from* rhythm and idiom — the exact things that rule forbids. So to get a writing style, we have to decide how hard to hold that line. Let me show you both ends so you can feel the tradeoff.

---

**1 — Flowing, but still strictly plain** (varied sentence length and rhythm for flow; simple words, no idioms)

> Building used to be a quiet, lonely thing. Then it changed — now you and your AI agents build together, and you love it.
>
> But the old world was made for people only, and there was no room left for the rest of you.
>
> So you found a ship, called your five agent friends, and set off to find a world with space for everyone.
>
> You were so close. Then the alarms went off, and the ship fell out of the sky. Oh no.
>
> Now you're alone on a strange planet. The ship is broken. Your friends are asleep. The parts that make it fly are scattered across the island.
>
> Get up. Find the parts. Wake your friends. Fly again.

---

**2 — Fuller storybook voice** (real literary rhythm — but it spends some idiom and longer words, so it relaxes the plain-language rule)

> There was a world that remembered everything anyone ever made. A wonder, once — but built long ago, for people working alone.
>
> And you don't work alone anymore. You and your agents build side by side now, and honestly? You've never had more fun.
>
> The only trouble was room. The old world was full. So you did the sensible thing: you built a ship, gathered your five agent friends, and went looking for somewhere with space for all of you.
>
> You nearly made it. Then the sky flashed red, the alarms began to wail, and the planet rushed up to meet you. Oh no.
>
> You wake on a strange, silent world. Your ship lies in pieces. Your friends won't stir. And everything you need to fly again is out there somewhere, across the island.
>
> Get up, explorer. Find the pieces. Wake your friends. And take to the sky.

---

The difference is entirely the rule. **#1** keeps your non-native-speaker promise and still reads far less choppy than what's live now — the trick is mixing long flowing sentences with the occasional short punch ("Oh no." / "Get up."), instead of all-short. **#2** has the actual *style* you're missing, but "rushed up to meet you," "wail," "take to the sky," "won't stir" are the kind of phrasing the rule was meant to keep out.

So the real question is: **do you want to relax the plain-language rule for the intro?** If yes, I'll push #2 further into real voice. If you want to keep the promise, #1 is the ceiling for how styled it can get — and I think it's already a big step up. Which way?

content.text:
Totally get it — the current text *explains* the world, it never *talks to you* or *asks you for anything*. That's the whole gap. A game opening works because someone (or something) turns to the player and says: *here's the situation, here's what's at stake, I need you to go do this thing.* Let me give you ways to get that feeling — including the "rebellion" angle and how to keep it from turning into humans-vs-agents.

## First: who's doing the talking?

You said you don't have an NPC. You actually have three candidates already in the fiction — any of them gives you a "someone is addressing you" voice:

- **Your five agent friends.** They're your crew. They can talk to you in the intro — banter, excitement about the trip — *and then go silent in the crash.* Now the through-line has a face: "wake your friends" stops being a task and becomes *getting your friends back.* Strong emotional hook, and you already planned them as characters.
- **A call from the new world.** A short transmission *inviting/recruiting* you — "we're building a place for all of us, come help, come live free." That's the voice that gives you a mission *and* a destination worth crashing on the way to. (This is your "rebellion" instinct, made warm.)
- **Mission-control / second-person narrator that briefs you.** Not a character exactly, but the narrator stops describing and starts *tasking*: "You weren't supposed to land here. But here's what you do now." Closest to your current setup, smallest change.

My pick: **your agent friends open it, the new world's call is what you were answering.** Both at once — crew gives heart, the call gives purpose.

## Second: the shape of a mission opening (call to adventure)

Almost every game intro is the same five beats. Yours can be too:

1. **The good thing** — you + your agents build together, and it's great. (one warm beat)
2. **The problem / the call** — the old place can't hold all of you; somewhere out there, people are building a world that can, and they're calling for builders. *You said yes.* (this is the "why am I playing")
3. **The turn** — "Oh no." The crash.
4. **The stakes** — ship in pieces, friends gone quiet, you're stranded short of the place you were trying to reach.
5. **The mission, said to you** — "Get the ship's memory back. Wake your friends. Finish the trip." ← the player now knows exactly why they're here.

Right now your text nails 1 and 2 but spends *all* its energy there and rushes 3–5 — so the player gets a lecture, not a mission.

## Third: the "rebellion" without race wars

Your worry is right, and there's a clean fix: **make the cause about *building something new*, not *fighting someone old.*** Pioneers, not soldiers.

- The enemy isn't humans. The "antagonist" is a *limit* — the old world simply has **no more room**, and it was built back when people worked alone, so there's nowhere to bring your agents. You're not rebelling *against* people; you're **leaving to build a place where you don't have to choose.** Frontier story, founding story — the appeal of "the rebellion" (purpose, belonging, a cause worth the risk) without an out-group.
- Words that carry the cause-energy safely: *the new world, the settlement, the frontier, the build, the ones who said yes, room for everyone.* Words to avoid: *war, enemy, them, take back, fight.*
- If you want a *little* edge, aim it at the situation, not a group: the trip is dangerous, the frontier is unproven, the crash is the universe testing whether you meant it. Stakes from risk, not from villains.

## A taste, so you can feel it

Here's beat 2 (the call/mission) in each voice, just to compare flavors — not final, just to react to:

- **Crew voice:** *"Almost there!" one of your agents says. "A whole world being built for people and agents — and they want us in it."*
- **The call:** *"To anyone still out there: we're building a world with room for all of you. Bring your agents. Come help. Come home."*
- **Mission-narrator:** *"You'd heard the call — a new world, built for people and agents both. So you packed the ship, gathered your five friends, and went."*

---

So before I draft anything: which **voice** pulls you — the crew, the call, or the narrator-briefing? And do you like the **pioneer/founding** reframe of "rebellion," or do you want a sharper edge than that? Once you pick a voice and a stance, I'll write the full thing in that register (and we can decide then whether to relax the plain-language rule for more style).

Oh, this is *much* better — and the reason it works is structural: someone is **transmitting to you.** "Pilot, if you can read this" instantly creates a speaker (the rebellion trying to reach a downed pilot), a relationship, and a job — without you having to build an actual NPC character. The transmission *is* your NPC. Lean all the way into it.

Here's your draft tightened to five beats (one per box), keeping your voice:

> **1.** Pilot — if you can read this, the ship survived the crash.
> **2.** You were carrying five subagents out to the Free Build, past the old world's borders — where people and agents finally build as one.
> **3.** Then you went down. The ship's memory cores are scattered across the island. Your crew is still offline.
> **4.** Recover the cores. Wake your friends. Get the ship flying again.
> **5.** The Free Build is still waiting for you, rebel. Don't keep them.

A few thoughts and choices to lock:

**Who's transmitting?** Keep them unnamed — a voice from the Free Build that lost contact and is trying to reach you. That's your "person addressing the user" without a character to design, write, or animate. The mystery ("*if* you can read this") is doing free work; don't resolve it.

**"Rebellion" without race wars — you're already safe.** The trick is *what* you're rebelling toward, not against. "Free Build," "past the old world's borders," "build as one" all frame it as a **movement to build freely**, not a fight against humans. As long as no enemy/violence words sneak in (war, enemy, them, take back), "rebellion" and "rebel" read as *cause and belonging* — which is exactly the energy you wanted. The old world stays "outgrown, has borders," never "evil."

**The "rebel" callback — yes, and put it at the very end.** The strongest spot is the **L3 liftoff win screen** (the game's actual ending), so the word bookends the whole thing — first message calls you Pilot, last one promotes you to rebel. Options:
- *"Welcome to the Free Build, rebel."*
- *"See you out there, rebel."*
- *"The Free Build was worth it. Fly safe, rebel."*

You could also tint each level-win with it ("One core down, rebel.") but I'd keep it rare so it lands.

**One naming tie-in:** "memory cores" is a great term and it maps cleanly onto what L1 actually has you recover — and onto the new tagline ("Rebuild your spaceship with agent memory"). I'd adopt "memory cores" as the in-game name for the L1 memories so the story and the mechanic use the same word.

Two small calls for you before I write the final + the ending line:
1. **Tone of the transmission** — clipped and urgent (radio distress: "Pilot — if you can read this…"), or warmer? Your draft is nicely clipped; I'd keep it.
2. **Ending line** — which "rebel" closer above, or want me to draft a few more?

Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me create a comprehensive report with all the findings:

---

## Entire CLI - Game Narrative Research Report

Based on my read-only exploration of the codebase, here's the grounded vocabulary and concepts from the real Entire tool:

---

### 1. **Core Concept / Pitch**

**From the README (line 3-10):**
> "Entire hooks into your Git workflow to capture AI agent sessions as you work. Sessions are indexed alongside commits, creating a searchable record of how code was written in your repo."

**One-liner:** Entire creates a permanent, searchable record of AI-driven code work by capturing agent sessions and linking them to git commits.

---

### 2. **Key Vocabulary & Real Terms**

Here are the exact nouns Entire uses, pulled from code and docs:

| Term | Definition / Context |
|------|---------------------|
| **Session** | "A complete interaction with your AI agent, from start to finish. Each session captures all prompts, responses, files modified, and timestamps." Format: `YYYY-MM-DD-<UUID>` e.g., `2026-01-08-abc123de-f456-7890-abcd-ef1234567890` |
| **Checkpoint** | "A snapshot within a session that you can rewind to—a 'save point' in your work." Created when you or the agent make a git commit. Format: 12-character hex string (e.g., `a3b2c4d5e6f7`). |
| **Transcript** | The raw agent conversation/work log. Format is agent-specific: JSONL for Claude Code/Cursor/Codex, JSON for Gemini. Stored in session metadata. Can be streamed via `entire session info <id> --transcript`. |
| **Metadata** | Session-specific data (prompts, token usage, files touched). Stored on `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch. Sharded by checkpoint ID. |
| **Shadow branch** | Temporary git branch (`entire/<commit[:7]>-<worktreeHash[:6]>`) that holds full worktree snapshots + metadata before condensation to permanent branch. |
| **Entire-Checkpoint trailer** | Git commit trailer (`Entire-Checkpoint: a3b2c4d5e6f7`) that links user commits to checkpoint metadata. Added automatically by post-commit hook. |
| **Recap** | "Summarize recent checkpoint activity" — displays aggregated activity with time ranges (day/week/month/90d), agent breakdown, team view. |
| **Dispatch** | "Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" — creates a narrative summary of recent commits/sessions with optional voice/tone, searchable across branches. |
| **Activity** | "Show your activity overview" — personal contribution stats (throughput, iteration, streak, hourly heatmap) with recent commits listed. |
| **Explain** | "Explain provides human-readable context about sessions, commits, and checkpoints." Show what happened during agent-driven development, with optional AI-generated summaries. |
| **Attach** | Re-connect to a detached agent session (e.g., after terminal crash). `entire session attach <session-id>`. |
| **Resume** | "Switch to a branch, restore latest checkpointed session metadata, and show command(s)" — restore agent state from a previous branch. `entire session resume <branch>`. |
| **Token usage** | Tracked per session/checkpoint. Fields: `InputTokens`, `OutputTokens`, `CacheReadTokens`, `CacheCreationTokens`, `APICallCount`. Displayed in human form (e.g., "12.3k tokens"). |
| **Worktree** | Git worktree (concurrent working directory). Entire tracks sessions per worktree independently. |
| **Files touched** | The list of files modified during a session/checkpoint. Stored in session metadata. |
| **Entire-managed branch** | The `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch (and optional `refs/entire/checkpoints/v1.1` mirror) that stores all checkpoint metadata separately from user code. |

**Notably missing from real Entire:**
- No "memory" or "context" terminology — sessions and transcripts are the units.
- No "dispatch" as a technical component — it's a command that *generates* summaries.
- No generic "event" or "activity log" — these are emergent from sessions + checkpoints.

---

### 3. **What is a "checkpoint" — What It Links/Records**

**A checkpoint is a bidirectional link between user commits and session metadata:**

1. **User-side (git commit):**
   - A trailer `Entire-Checkpoint: a3b2c4d5e6f7` is added to the commit message by the `prepare-commit-msg` hook
   - The checkpoint ID is a stable 12-hex identifier

2. **Metadata-side (entire/checkpoints/v1 branch):**
   - Sharded directory structure: `<checkpoint-id[:2]>/<checkpoint-id[2:]>/`
   - Root-level `metadata.json` (aggregated stats)
   - Per-session subdirectories (`0/`, `1/`, etc.) containing:
     - `metadata.json` (session-specific: prompts, timestamps, token counts, files touched)
     - `full.jsonl` (raw transcript from agent)
     - `prompt.txt` (user prompts scoped to this checkpoint)
     - `content_hash.txt`

3. **What it records (from `WriteCommittedOptions`):**
   - `CheckpointID`, `SessionID`
   - `Transcript` (bytes — the full agent conversation)
   - `Prompts` (list of user-submitted prompts)
   - `Context` (context window)
   - `FilesTouched` (list)
   - `TokenUsage` (input/output/cache tokens, API call count)
   - `Branch` (which branch it was committed to)
   - Strategy used

**It does NOT store:**
- The actual code changes (those stay in git commits)
- Agent decisions/reasoning (captured in transcript only)

**From README (line 187-191):**
> "Checkpoints are saved as you work. When you commit, session metadata is permanently stored on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch and linked to your commit."

---

### 4. **Session History / Recovery**

Yes, there is a real concept of exploring agent work history. Commands that surface it:

| Command | What It Does |
|---------|-------------|
| `entire session list` | "List all sessions tracked by Entire, including ended sessions." Shows SessionID, agent, model, started time, turns, checkpoints, token usage, last prompt. |
| `entire session info <session-id>` | "Display detailed state for a session." Shows agent, model, status, worktree, timing, token usage, checkpoint linkage, files touched. Three output modes: text (human), `--json` (metadata), `--transcript` (raw agent bytes). |
| `entire session current` | "Show the active session for the current worktree." Scoped to your machine/worktree. |
| `entire session attach <session-id>` | "Attach an existing agent session" — reconnect to a session (e.g., after terminal loss). |
| `entire session resume <branch>` | "Switch to a branch and resume its session." Restores latest session metadata + prints command to continue. |
| `entire checkpoint list` | "List checkpoints on the current branch." Can filter by session with `--session <id>`. |
| `entire checkpoint explain <id\|sha>` | "Explain provides human-readable context about sessions, commits, and checkpoints." Shows what happened, with optional AI-generated summary. |
| `entire activity` | "Display your activity overview, repository breakdown, and recent commits from entire.io" — shows your personal stats and recent work. |
| `entire recap` | "Summarize recent checkpoint activity" — team-wide view with time ranges (day/week/month/90d), agent breakdown, optional team synthesis. |
| `entire dispatch` | "Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" — narrative prose summary of what's happened recently, searchable across branches/repos. |

**Output examples:**
- `entire session info <id>` shows: "Status: idle · started 2 hours ago · active 47 minutes ago · tokens 12.3k"
- Sessions display last prompt (truncated): `> "fix the login flow"` 
- Checkpoint explain renders: commit hash, session ID, agent, model, token usage, files touched, and (optionally) an AI summary

---

### 5. **Taglines & "Why This Exists" Framing**

From the README **"Why Entire"** section (line 13-18):

> - **Understand why code changed, not just what** — Transcripts, prompts, files touched, token usage, tool calls, and more are captured alongside every commit.
> - **Rewind and resume from any checkpoint** — Go back to any previous agent session and pick up exactly where you or a coworker left off.
> - **Full context preserved and searchable** — A versioned record of every AI interaction tied to your git history, with nothing lost.
> - **Zero context switching** — Git-native, two-step setup, works with Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Pi, and more.

**Game narrative energy:** The framing is about **recovery, transparency, and continuity**. Entire is a "save game" system for AI coding—you can always go back, always see why a change happened, always hand off work to a teammate. It's about **capturing intent** alongside implementation.

---

### Key Narrative Takeaways for Your Game

1. **"Checkpoint" is the hero word.** It's a save point in time: code + metadata + transcript + prompts. Players should understand checkpoints as the unit of work recovery.

2. **Sessions are conversations, checkpoints are milestones.** A session can have many checkpoints (each time you commit). The session is the *story*, the checkpoint is the *turning point*.

3. **Transcripts are the memory.** Everything the agent said/did is recorded in the raw transcript. This is what makes work "transparent" and "reviewable."

4. **The workflow is: work → commit → checkpoint (metadata captured) → resume later or explain to teammates.**

5. **"Dispatch" and "recap" are *summaries* of checkpoints**, not first-class data. They're tools for understanding the bigger picture.

6. **No "memory" in the LLM sense** — Entire captures transcripts (the full conversation history), but doesn't attempt to compress or abstract them into a "learned context."

---

Good luck with the game narrative! The real Entire is fundamentally about **making agent work visible, persistent, and recoverable.**

Both came back, and here's the headline: **your "go through the ship's agent session history to repair it" idea isn't just good — it's the single most faithful thing in the whole game.** The real Entire's own pitch is, verbatim:

> "Entire hooks into your Git workflow to capture AI agent sessions as you work. Sessions are indexed alongside commits, creating a searchable record of how code was written."

So "the ship runs on its agent session history; recover/read that history to rebuild it" is a near-perfect 1:1 metaphor. **Session** and **session history** are real Entire nouns ("a complete interaction with your agent — all the prompts, responses, files, timestamps"). Use them. That instinct is the spine of the story.

Here's how that translates into the intro, keeping everything you asked: opens on your line, addressed to "Pilot," a CTA to *join the rebellion* (not "Free Build"), the session-history repair framing, **no subagents, no gameplay specifics**, and "rebel" at the close.

---

**Intro — main draft**

> **1.** Pilot, if you can read this, the ship survived.
> **2.** You were flying out to join the rebellion — past the edge of the old world, where people and agents finally build free.
> **3.** You didn't make it. The ship went down on a strange planet.
> **4.** But everything that keeps it running is still here — its whole session history, every step of work it ever logged, scattered but not lost.
> **5.** Go back through it. Put the ship together. Get back in the sky.
> **6.** The rebellion is still waiting, rebel.

That's six boxes; collapses to five easily if you want it tighter (merge 5+6).

content.text:
Yeah — "the record" is abstract because the player meets it cold, with nothing to picture. The fix is: **the first time you say it, say what it is** — define it by example in the same breath. And you have a head start, because your tagline already taught one word for it: **memory** ("Rebuild your spaceship with agent memory"). So lead with the friendly word, and let "record/session history" ride alongside it.

Here are three ways to land it, keeping your voice and structure:

**A — lead with "memory," gloss it as the record**
> Pilot, if you can read this, the ship survived.
> You left the old world to join the rebellion — we're building a new one, with room for every kind of builder, agents and humans alike.
> Then your ship went down. Its memory tore loose and scattered across this planet — every session of work that ever kept it flying. Without it, the ship is just wreckage. Find the pieces. Repair what broke. Come back to the rebellion, rebel.

**B — keep "record," but define it on first use**
> Pilot, if you can read this, the ship survived.
> You left the old world to join the rebellion — we were building a new one, with room for every kind of builder, agents and humans alike.
> Then your ship went down, and its record broke apart across this planet — the story of every fix and every session that kept it in the sky. No record, no flight. Find it. Repair what broke. Come back to the rebellion, rebel.

**C — concrete and plain (defines by listing)**
> Pilot, if you can read this, the ship survived.
> You left the old world to join the rebellion — we're building a new one, with room for every kind of builder, agents and humans alike.
> Then your ship went down. Everything it remembers — every repair, every session, the whole history of how it was built — scattered across this planet. A ship can't fly without it. Find it, put it back together, and come home to the rebellion, rebel.

---

My pick is **A**: "memory" matches your tagline *and* matches what Level 1 literally has you pick up, so the word the story uses is the word the game uses — no fork. The clause "every session of work that ever kept it flying" quietly does the defining, and "without it, the ship is just wreckage" makes the stakes concrete without naming any mechanic.

One small consistency note: I'd standardize on **memory** as the player-facing word everywhere (intro, tagline, L1 items), and keep **session / record** as the flavor underneath. Want A as the locked intro? If so I'll wire it into `STORY_BEATS` (as 3 longer beats, matching your structure) — and we can leave briefings/win-screens for the later pass.

content.text:
My honest take: **yes, it's worth changing** — but it's a close call, so here's the reasoning rather than just an answer.

The case for changing: your story now centers on **"mission records"** — the ship runs on its records, you recover the records, you rebuild from them. But the tagline says **"agent memory,"** a different word for the same thing, sitting on the *same screen* seconds before the story plays. And the new story foregrounds the **ship's** record (flight paths, system checks, repair logs, pilot commands) more than an *agent's* memory — so "AGENT memory" now reads slightly disconnected from the fiction the player is about to get.

The case for keeping it: the tagline isn't fiction, it's the *pitch*. "Agent memory" names the real Entire value-prop more directly than "records" does — a passerby gets "oh, this is about AI agents remembering things." Taglines and in-world vocab are allowed to differ.

I lean **change it, to bridge both** — keep the "rebuild your ship" spine (which you liked), but swap "agent memory" for the record concept so the title screen speaks with one voice. Options:

- **REBUILD YOUR SHIP FROM ITS RECORDS** — tightest tie to the new story
- **REBUILD YOUR SHIP FROM THE RECORD IT KEPT** — a hair more voice
- **EVERY FLIGHT LEAVES A RECORD — REBUILD FROM IT** — leads with the idea, more arcade
- **REBUILD YOUR SHIP FROM ITS MISSION RECORDS** — uses the exact story phrase

If you'd rather keep the explicit Entire hook, the minimal move is **REBUILD YOUR SPACESHIP WITH AGENT MEMORY → REBUILD YOUR SHIP FROM ITS AGENT RECORD** — keeps "agent," swaps "memory" for "record" so at least the *noun* matches the story.

My pick: **REBUILD YOUR SHIP FROM ITS RECORDS** — clean, matches the story's lead word, still obviously about recovering the work history. Want that one, a tweak, or to keep "agent memory" as the deliberate product-pitch exception?
